titleStmt.title: The effects of three types of balance training programs on measures of balance and muscle power in prepubertal children: A randomized controlled trial

analytic.title: The effects of three types of balance training programs on measures of balance and muscle power in prepubertal children: A randomized controlled trial

biblStruct.note: Received 16 July 2023 Received in revised form 29 September 2023 Accepted 6 October 2023

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: b64991a

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=0, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=true, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], flavor=null

textClass.keywords: Training intensity Dose-response relation Youth Jumping performance

div.p: The purpose of this study was to investigate the effects of static (SBT), quasi-dynamic (QDBT), and dynamic (DBT) balance training on balance and muscle power performance in prepubertal children. Design: Randomized, controlled trial design. Methods: Fifty-six children (10 and 11 years) were randomly assigned to static (n = 14), quasi-dynamic (n = 14) and dynamic balance training (n = 15), or a control group (n = 13). One-legged static (i.e., unipedal stance on stable surface), quasi-dynamic (i.e., unipedal stance on unstable surface), and dynamic balance (i.e., jumping and stabilizing in unipedal stance on unstable surface) were assessed using two dependent variables, i.e., center of pressure surface area (CoP SA) and velocity (CoP V) with opened (OE) and closed (CE) eyes; as well as lower limbs muscle power (i.e., standing long jump test [SLJ] and countermovement jump [CMJ height]). Results: A significant group × test interactions were observed for static (CoP SA OE; p < 0.05, d = 1.18), quasi-dynamic (CoP SA CE; p < 0.05, d = 0.80), dynamic (CoP V CE; p < 0.05, d = 0.87) balance and power (SLJ [p < 0.05, d = 0.87] and CMJ height [p < 0.01, d = 2.66]) tests. Post hoc analysis showed that DBT demonstrated greater improvement in the measure of balance (p < 0.05, d = 0.80 to 5.17) and power (p < 0.05, d = 2.66 to 3.65) performance. Conclusions: To enhance balance and muscle power performance in prepubertal children, it appears that the level of difficulty in balance training may influence the observed effects.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Evidence-based prescriptions for balance training (BT) in youth have previously been established. [1][2][3][4][5] It is well known that BT has the potential to induce performance improvements in selected components of physical fitness (e.g., balance, muscle strength/power, change of direction speed). [3][4][5][6] Regarding pediatric population, the relationships between age/puberty and stages of postural balance system development were evidenced. [7][8][9] In this context, it was reported that children between the ages of four and five years of age employing large and rapid postural adjustment strategies. 10 By the age of seven years, the development of the structures responsible for motor control is complete. 11 From 8 to 10 years of age, reductions in speed and increases in precision of adjustments are clearly seen, as there is better integration between the open and closed-loop control systems to maintain this control. 9,10 According to Roncesvalles et al., 8 by the age of 10 years, children will have achieved postural stability similar to adults. Thus, given that balance and coordination are not fully developed in children aged between 10 and 11 years, 12 there is a critical need to foster and enhance balance capabilities during this particular stage.

To maximize the effectiveness of BT, it is crucial to elucidate the optimal dosage of different programming parameters (e.g., training frequency, volume, intensity). While training frequency and volume can easily be manipulated in BT, it is not the case for balance intensity. An often load dimension is "task difficulty", which may be regarded as an easily implemented tool for quantifying BT intensity. 3,4,13,14 Indeed, changing the level of balance task difficulty (e.g., varying stance conditions or manipulating the sensory input) has been described as a vehicle to design varied and challenging BT programs. In this context, manipulating the base of support (BoS) and/or sensory inputs (proprioceptive and visual) has been considered as one way for balance task difficulty adaptation. 15 While recent intervention studies have investigated the effects of balance exercises or balance training (BT) with varying task difficulties on static and proactive balance in youth, 2,5,8 it remains unclear whether diverse static, quasi-dynamic, and dynamic balance training programs yield similar training adaptations in prepubertal individuals.

Additionally, there is evidence of facilitative effects from long-term BT when task difficulty is adequately manipulated. 4,13,17,18 In this regard, Schedler et al. 4 reported significant increases in dynamic (d = 0.35) and proactive (d = 0.60) balance with reduced BoS (i.e., high task difficulty), after 8 weeks of BT using increasing balance task difficulty (high vs low balance task with regard to manipulating the BoS). Similarly, Blasco et al. 16 indicated that varying unstable surfaces, by modifying balance task difficulty, resulted in similar improvement of the static and dynamic domains of balance, and functional performances in young adults. With regard to the aforementioned studies, it seems that there is a lack of an effectiveness mean to manipulate balance training intensity, particularly in prepubertal children.

In addition, a growing number of studies 19,20 have clearly shown that BT-related adaptations are highly task-specific. However, information about how the type of balance exercise, with increased levels of difficulty (i.e., static, quasi-dynamic, and dynamic type) can moderate the transfer effect to muscle strength and power performance outcomes after the long-term BT is limited. Based on the currently available evidence in the existing literature, we can speculate that increasing balance task difficulty using a variety of dynamic exercise, may yield long-term effects on athletic performance.

Therefore, the purpose of this study was to examine the effects of short-term static (SBT) versus quasi-dynamic (QDBT) versus dynamic (DBT) BT on static, quasi-dynamic, dynamic balance, and lower limbs muscle power in prepubertal children. Based on earlier longitudinal studies 3,4,16,21 we hypothesized that all BT programs would result in balance and muscle power improvements in prepubertal children. Furthermore, given that prepubertal children rely on a premature postural control system 5 ; and that BT-related adaptations are highly taskspecific 4,16,20 we expected that children would benefit more from dynamic BT (high task difficulty), due to the multiple postural control strategies involved in such training.

div.head: Participants
div.p: Based on the study of Schedler et al. 18 on the effects of balance training conducted under a high versus a low level of task difficulty on balance performance, an a priori power analysis with a type I error rate of 0.05 and 80 % statistical power was computed. The analysis indicated that overall, 48 participants would be sufficient to observe significant, large-sized acute effects (Cohen's f ≥ 0.5) for static balance performance (i.e., static one-legged stance time). In addition, to account for potential loss of participants to attrition, we added 10 % to our total target sample, equating to 53.

Inclusion criteria included healthy, normoweight prepubertal children with a history of regular attendance to physical education lessons (twice a week). Additionally, participants should not be engaged in any afterschool activities or formalized strength and conditioning training programs. Finally, 56 prepubertal children, aged between 10 and 11 years, from the same public school, volunteered to participate in this study.

All participants were randomly assigned to three different exercise groups by a staff member not involved in the measurements through a computer-generated method (https://random.org/lists). A parallel design with a stratified randomization method according to gender was used.

The three groups were: Static Balance Training (SBT; n = 14; age = 10.75 ± 0.23 yrs, height = 147.50 ± 5.91 cm, body mass = 41.39 ± 2.39 kg, PHV = -2.26 ± 0.29), Quasi-Dynamic Balance Training (QDBT; n = 15; age = 10.70 ± 0.32 yrs, height = 145.87 ± 8.18 cm, body mass = 41.57 ± 2.08 kg, PHV: -2.36 ± 0.41), Dynamic Balance Training (DBT; n = 14; age = 10.67 ± 1.8 yrs, height = 148.36 ± 7.71 cm, body mass = 41.29 ± 1.53 kg, PHV = -2.28 ± 0.35), and a control group (CON; n = 13; age = 10.65 ± 0.28 yrs, height = 149.54 ± 5.90 cm, body mass = 41.30 ± 1.54 kg, PHV = -2.25 ± 0.30), in order to avoid learning effects and fatigue.

All participating children came from families of similar socioeconomic backgrounds and had identical daily school schedules. All training interventions (i.e., SBT, QDBT, and DBT) were integrated into the two weekly physical education classes of participants. Before the start of the study, participants were given a letter that included written information about the experimental procedure with a parental consent request. Parental and subject informed consent was obtained after a thorough explanation of the objectives and scope, procedures, risks (i.e., pain, injury, illness or disease brought about by the methods and procedures of the current study), and benefits of the study. Participants were free to withdraw at any time if they felt uncomfortability or pain during the procedures or they did not want to participate anymore. The study was conducted according to the latest version of the Declaration of Helsinki and the protocol was fully approved by the Local Clinical Research Ethics Committee "Personal Protection Committee" under the following code (N°: 0365//2021) before the commencement of any assessments. None of the participating children had a history of musculoskeletal, neurological, or orthopedic disorders that might have impaired their ability to execute the prescribed tasks/tests. All the procedures were conducted following the Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials (CONSORT 2022, Appendix 1) and Better reporting of interventions: template for intervention description and replication (TIDieR, Appendix 2) checklists.

div.head: Procedures
div.p: According to the study aim, all the procedures were conducted at Gafsa (Tunisia) primary school coinciding with the second half of the academic year (April-June 2021). All training interventions (SBT, QDBT, and DBT) were integrated into the two weekly physical education lessons of the participants, which were carried out in the morning, at the same time and with the same staff. The measurements were specific to purpose of trial, they were not part of the typical curriculum contents. The same certified strength and conditioning coaches supervised face-to-face all the testing/intervention procedures sessions, which were conducted in groups. The staff in charge of the measurements and analyses was blinded from the group allocation. The attendance rate was controlled by directly counting the assistants. Before the start of the study, all participants in both the interventions and control groups completed a 2-week familiarization period (3 sessions a week) to become acquainted with the environment, session structure, equipment, techniques used in each balance and power test, as well as the BT exercises.

Balance (primary outcome) and power (secondary outcome) performance were tested before and following the 6-week SBT (i.e., tandem stance with OE and CE), QDBT (tandem stance on unstable surface with OE and CE) and DBT (i.e., Jumping and standing on tandem stance on balance pad with OE and CE) on period in an indoor sport hall of the local public primary schools. The balance was the primary outcome of the study as it was directly associated with the training interventions. Additionally, jumping performance (power) was selected as a secondary outcome to assess potential positive transfer of balance training to jumping performance. The data collection sessions started 48 h after the familiarization period, where all prepubertal children received an orientation where the static (center of pressor surface with opened eyes [CoP SA OE]), quasi-dynamic (center of pressor surface with closed eyes [CoP SA CE]) and dynamic (center of pressor velocity with closed eyes [CoP V CE]) balance and power (standing long jump [SLJ] and countermovement jump [CMJ]) tests were explained. Body height, body mass, body composition, balance (static, quasi-dynamic and dynamic balance tests) and muscle power performance of lower limbs (horizontal and vertical jump tests) were assessed. All subjects conducted a standardized 10-min warm-up prior to testing, that consisted of submaximal running (e.g., skipping, hip in/out) and dynamic stretching.

The body height and the body mass were collected using a wallmounted stadiometer (i.e., Florham Park, NJ) and an electronic scale (i.e., Baty International, West Sussex, England), respectively. The sum of skinfolds was assessed using Harpenden skinfold calipers. Body fat percentage was therefore estimated using the equation of Deurenberg et al. 22 (Body fat percentage = 222.23 + 26.56 3 log (Biceps, triceps, subscapular, and suprailiac)), who reported similar prediction errors between adults and adolescents. Biological maturity was evaluated, non-invasively, by incorporating measures of chronological age and body height into a regression equation to subsequently predict biological age from pic height velocity (PHV), as described by Moore et al. 23

div.head: Primary outcome: balance performance
div.p: Balance tests were performed with eyes opened (EO) and closed (EC) using a force plate with three strain gauges and a sampling rate of 40 Hz (PostureWin©, Techno Concept ®, Cereste, France) according to the procedure described by Hammami et al. 17 Throughout all balance tests, participants were instructed to look straight ahead at a cross, placed at eye level on a nearby wall (2 m distance). Each test trial lasted 30 s. As dependent variables, two center of pressure (CoP) sway parameters were analyzed: COP surface area (CoP SA) in mm 2 and CoP velocity (CoP V) in mm/ms). Similarly, each test trial lasted 30 s. The domains namely static (center of pressor surface with opened eyes [CoP SA OE]), quasi-dynamic (center of pressor surface with closed eyes [CoP SA CE]) and dynamic (center of pressor velocity with closed eyes [CoP V CE]) balance were analyzed. CoP SA represents the ellipse of the area covered by the trajectory of the CoP whereas the CoP V indicates the total distances covered by the CoP divided by the duration of the sampled period. 4 For the balance parameters, the lower the value, the better the balance performance. 24 Two trials were performed with approximately 2 min of recovery and the best result was used for analysis.

div.head: Static balance test.
div.p: For the static balance test, participants stood in a single leg stance on the dominant leg for 30 s with EO, on a stable surface. The leg used to kick a soccer ball identified the dominant leg 25 (Fig. 1, a).

2.2.1.2. Quasi-dynamic balance test. For the quasi-dynamic balance test, participant stood on the stabliometric platform with unipodal leg stance on the dominant leg with EC, on unstable surface (i.e., BOSU) (Fig. 1, b).

div.head: Dynamic balance test.
div.p: For the dynamic balance test, participants jumped with EC onto the stabliometric platform where a BOSU ball was placed upside-down and stabilized the dominant leg on the unstable surface (i.e., BOSU) (Fig. 1, c).

div.head: Secondary outcome: muscle power performance
div.p: Each participant performed a bilateral standing horizontal long jump (SLJ) test where the participants stood stationary with the toes aligned level with the start line and were instructed to push off vigorously and jump forward as far as possible. Participants were allowed the use of a countermovement with arms and body swing. The distance (cm) jumped from the start line at takeoff to the position of the heel upon landing was measured in centimeters using a metal tape measure. Participants completed three trials, with the highest score being recorded.

The countermovement jump (CMJ) test was performed using an Ergojump ® system (ErgojumpP apparatus, Globus Italia, Codogne, Italy), which recorded jump height (cm). Each test was separated by a passive recovery period of at least 5 min. The CMJ involved the participants descending from an upright standing position until a knee flexion angle of 90°, followed immediately by a vertical jump. Participants were encouraged to perform the eccentric phase of the jump as quickly as possible to maximize jump height. Two trials were performed, with approximately 2 min recovery, and the best result was used for analysis.

div.head: Training programs
div.p: After, assessment, all experimental groups conducted 6 weeks of BT with 2 sessions per week (30-35 min each) that included six balance exercises per training session (Table 1). The duration and frequency of the programs were based on a previous study that found significant improvements in balance with large to very large effect sizes of 6 weeks and frequency between 1 and 3 days per week. 3 After a 15-min warmup program, including general (e.g., skipping, running with hip in/out) and specific (e.g., two-/one-legged stance on unstable devices, forward/ backward beam walking) exercises, two sets per balance exercise were performed for 30 s each with a 60 s rest period between sets and a 90 s break between exercises. While participants of the SBT group performed all balance exercises with a low level of task difficulty, subjects in the QDBT and DBT groups executed the same or similar exercises using a quasi-dynamic and dynamic high level of task difficulty,

div.head: Data analysis
div.p: Data are presented as means and standard deviations (SD). After a basic data curation, normality was assessed and confirmed using the Shapiro-Wilk test. No outcome data were missing or excluded from the study. The data were then analyzed using a 4 (groups: SBT, NDBT, DBT, CON) by 2 (time: pre, post) analysis of variance (ANOVA) for repeated measures. Where the assumption of sphericity was violated, Greenhouse-Geisser correction was used to interpret the results. Additionally, effect sizes (ES) were determined from ANOVA output by converting partial eta-squared to Cohen's d. Moreover, within-group ES were computed using the following equation: ES = (mean postmean pre) / SD. In accordance with Hopkins, 26 ES were considered to be either 'trivial' (< 0.2) 'small' (> 0.2-0.6), 'moderate' (> 0.6-1.2), 'large' (> 1.2-2), or 'very large' (> 2). Test re-test reliability of the variables was assessed using Cronbach's model of ICCs and SEMs according to the method of Peltola. 27 The level of significance was set, a priori, at p < 0.05. The statistical analysis was carried out using IBM SPSS (version 25, IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, United States) and R Core Team 33 .

div.head: Results
div.p: The sample was composed by 56 prepubertal children, including 31 males (68.42 %) and 25 females (31.58 %). As planned, all of them completed the study (Fig. 2). The rate of attendance among the participants was 100 %. No missing sessions were recorded. No training or testingrelated adverse events were reported. There were no statistically significant differences in baseline values between groups. In addition, no significant between-group pre-training differences were found for any of the analyzed parameters. Participants' characteristics are included in Table 2.

div.head: Reliability of the applied tests
div.p: Intra-class correlation coefficients (ICCs) showed good reliability for all tests. ICC-values ranged from 0.79 to 0.95, with a standard error of measurement (SEM) from 0.47 to 12.03 and a coefficient of variation of <5 %. Furthermore, a paired t-test showed no significant differences between the scores recorded during the two trials for all measured variables. ). However, the CG did not reveal any significant improvement from pretest to posttest (p = 0.13, d = 0.77) (Table 4).

div.head: Quasi-dynamic balance performance
div.p: A significant main effect of time was found for the quasidynamic balance variable (CoP SA CE) (p < 0.001, d = 1.21). A significant group × time interactions were detected for the CoP SA CE only (p < 0.04, d = 0.80). Post hoc analyses indicated very large improvement (p = 0.001, d = 2.55) in CoP SA CE performance from pretest to posttest in favor of the DBT. However, the CG did not reveal any significant improvement from pretest to posttest (p = 0.43, d = 0.46) (Table 4).

div.head: Dynamic balance performance
div.p: A significant main effect of time was established for the dynamic balance variable (CoP V CE) (p < 0.001; d = 1.56). A significant group × time interactions were found for CoP V CE (p < 0.05, d = 0.87). Post hoc analysis showed very large improvements in CoP V CE performance from pretest to posttest in favor of the DBT group (p < 0.01, d = 2.35) with comparison to the QDBT (p < 0.01, d = 2.25) and the SBT (p < 0.01, d = 2.16) groups. However, the CG did not reveal any significant improvement from pretest to posttest (p = 0.82, d = 0.40) (Table 4).

div.head: Secondary outcome: effects on muscle power performance
Standing long jump performance
div.p: Statistical calculations revealed a significant main effect of time for SLJ (p < 0.01, d = 2.84) and a significant group × test interaction (p < 0.01, d = 1.70) (Fig. 4). Post hoc analysis showed very large improvement in SLJ performance from pretest to posttest in favor of the DBT group (p < 0.01, d = 3.55). However, the CG did not reveal any significant improvement from pretest to posttest (p > 0.05, d = 0.02) (Table 4).

div.head: Countermovement jump performance
div.p: A significant main effect of time was established for the CMJ height (p = 0.0001; d = 4.76) and a significant group × time interactions (p = 0.0001, d = 2.66) were also noted. Post hoc analysis showed very large enhancements from pretest to posttest in favor of the DBT group (p < 0.01, d = 6.78). However, the CG did not reveal any significant improvement from pretest to posttest (p > 0.05, d = 0.45) (Table 4).

div.head: Discussion
div.p: This study was an investigation of the effects of three 6-weeks BT programs, involving a static (SBT), quasi-dynamic (QDBT), and dynamic (DBT) BT exercises on static (CoP SAOE, primary outcome), quasidynamic (CoP SA CE, secondary outcome), and dynamic (CoP V CE, secondary outcome) balance and power (SLJ and CMJ, secondary outcome) performance in children. Based on similar crosssectional 3,14,28,29 and longitudinal 4,16,21 studies and with regard to our research hypothesis, the main finding was that DBT would show the largest benefits as the DBT tasks are highly dynamic compared with QDBT, SBT, and CON on balance (primary outcome) and power (secondary outcome) parameters. Therefore, it is likely that similar to young adult 29 and adolescent, 4,30 children responded positively to a bout of DBT exercise, which consequently improved their balance and power capacities more than when performing slow balance exercises intensity (i.e., QDBT and SBT). This study was the first to report the longitudinal effects of increasing balance task difficulty on the measure of static, quasi-dynamic, and static balance and power performance in children being aged 10-11 years old.

Partly in accordance with our hypothesis, we detected greater improvements in the main outcome variable (i.e., balance performance, CoP SA and CoP V) for the group that used a high (i.e., DBT) compared to quasi (QDBT) and low (i.e., SBT) balance task difficulty during BT. There is empirical evidence 4,21,29,30 that balance performance can be challenged by the level of balance task difficulty. In this regard, Schedler et al. 4 showed that following 7 weeks of BT, BT-high appears to be more effective for increasing measures of proactive (Y-Balance Test [YBT] reach distance, Functional Reach Test [FRT]; Timed-Up-and-Go Test [TUG]) balance (Δ + 8 %, p < 0.001, d = 0.35) in adolescent. Similar, Murillo et al. 29 assessed the effect of increasing difficulty in a standing balance task in young adults. The amplitude of postural sway significantly increased as the level of task difficulty changed from standing on a static surface over standing on a surface with medium followed by high instability. Further, Blasco et al. 16 determined the effect of BT with different stability conditions on balance performance in young adults. Participants were assigned to BT groups that trained on stable ground (corresponds to BT-low) or on unstable surfaces (corresponds to BThigh). Following 3 weeks of treatment (3 sessions per week), both groups improved their balance performance (i.e., YBT and FRT reach distance, one-legged stance time).

Thus, regularly performing balance tasks of dynamic as compared to quasi-dynamic and static BT allows larger adaptive processes, which explains the larger improvements we observed in the DBT compared to the QDBT and SBT groups. Furthermore, DBT may elicit larger changes in sensory integration and postural control strategy compared to QDBT and SBT. In this regard, Gebel et al. 30 showed that the activity of selected brain areas (e.g., bilateral central) was affected by the increasing of the postural demands. Additionally, following 6 weeks of balance training on an unstable surface (i.e., slackline), Giboin et al. 21 reported that the decrease of the H-reflex or the Hoffmann's reflex (which is defined by the reflectory reaction of muscles after electrical stimulation of sensory fibers) amplitudes during stepping on a slackline indicated a more anticipatory/feedforward organization of postural control. Another explanation relates to the central nervous plane. 30,31 For example, Gebel et al. 30 used electroencephalographic analyses to examine the impact of increasing balance task difficulty on cortical activity in adolescents. They observed significant increases in the theta frequency band power and decreases in the alpha-2 frequency band power with increasing levels of task difficulty. The authors interpreted their finding as indicating enhanced attentional processes and/or error monitoring as well as increased sensory information processing due to increasing postural demands. Furthermore, participants in the DBT group exclusively engaged in dynamic balance exercises. As a result, they were primarily assessed for dynamic balance performance, although evaluations of other abilities such as static and quasi-dynamic balance performance were also conducted. Consequently, it would be reasonable to anticipate a 'learning effect' in which improvements in one ability, such as dynamic balance performance, could positively influence other dimensions like static and quasi-dynamic balance performance. This 'learning effect' could explain the greater improvements observed in balance and overall performance following DBT compared to QDBT and SBT. It implies an enhanced adaptive capacity, contributing to the superior performance of the DBT group compared to the QDBT and SBT among children. The results of the present study demonstrated that DBT yielded in a greater improvement in horizontal and vertical jump performance (i.e., SLJ and CMJ height), in children. Recently, Gebel et al., 3 in their narrative review, showed that BT has the potential to induce transfer effects to selected health parameters (e.g., muscle strength/ power) in youth. The authors argued that BT-induced neuromuscular adaptations might selectively be beneficial for explosive type of muscle actions (i.e., CMJ height and SLJ). Results from the present study could be explained by the fact that the test measures were performed within a more limited base of support (CMJ test) or with the center of gravity being moved outside the base of support (SLJ, CMJ), which would be affected to a much greater extent by DBT. In explaining the present results, it is important to acknowledge that we studied prepubertal children, who frequently performed dynamic balance tasks (i.e., tandem and hopping and landing with bipedal and unipedal) in their daily life activities and their physical exercise lessons. Since precise control of postural sway is needed for dynamic movement, children with enhanced control of postural sway can better control their body position or center of mass over their BoS. 2,12 Furthermore, the present results may be attributed to the improved proprioceptive afferent feedback and the anticipatory postural adjustments 2,30 and motor coordination and to the decreased postural sway parameters (greater stability) among other balance-related mechanisms. Furthermore, as maturation has been shown to contribute to the trainability of balance, 2,30 the rate at which these individuals are experiencing maturation may, in part, explain the superiority DBTresponses found in our study. Hence, using high balance training task difficulty (i.e., DBT), children with immature balance capabilities and less developed neuromuscular system 12 would conceivably respond positively to DBT. Because children's motor skills are still emerging, 2,32 this could be related to the general adaptive mechanisms that occur in the level of the spinal and supraspinal centers following DBT in children.

Although we present a novel addition to the literature, this study is not without limitations, and should be mentioned, accordingly. First, the sample of participants involved comprised pre-pubertal school children. Therefore, the results obtained may only be generalized to similar samples of participants. Second, balancing on the stabilometer is a task with high internal, but low external validity, which limits the transferability of the present results on balance tasks performed during everyday life or sports. Moreover, future studies need to elucidate cortical activity during the performance of balance tasks, with increasing difficulty, using robust measures, such as electroencephalography. Lastly, we were unable to quantify the level of fatigue immediately following the implementation of the balance training (BT) exercise programs, which could potentially have influenced the observed effects on balance and muscle power. Future studies could include assessments of Ratings of Perceived Exertion (RPE) within a few minutes after the execution of Static Balance Training (SBT), Quasi-Dynamic Balance Training (QDBT), and Dynamic Balance Training (DBT) exercises to determine whether fatigue may have played a role in mitigating the observed effects.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: The present study, to the best of our knowledge, is the first to investigate the effects of Static Balance Training (SBT), Quasi-Dynamic Balance Training (QDBT), and Dynamic Balance Training (DBT) on static, quasi-dynamic, and dynamic balance, as well as lower limb muscle power in prepubertal children.

In summary, the most relevant findings indicate that DBT resulted in significantly greater improvements in balance and muscle power performance compared to QDBT and SBT. These results suggest that prepubertal children exhibit heightened responsiveness to DBT for enhancing balance and power performance. This insight can be instrumental in optimizing individualized balance training regimens and fostering longterm motor skill development in this age group.

As balance and muscle power performance are often integrated into physical education courses, the findings of this study hold potential for incorporation into regular physical education curricula. Scientists and practitioners should consider the outcomes of this research when working with children and implementing balance exercises. Consequently, for those aiming to improve balance and jumping performance in children, Dynamic Balance Training (DBT) should be taken into account.

Although our study involved children in a physical education class, it is conceivable that Static Balance Training (SBT) and Quasi-Dynamic Balance Training (QDBT) exercises might be better suited for obese children due to their lower training loads during balance training. Further research in this area is warranted. CRediT authorship contribution statement R.H., L.A. and H.R. were involved in conceptualization and methodology. R.H. and L.A. conducted the study. P.G., J.G.M. and J.C.C. analyzed and interpreted the data. R.H. drafted the manuscript. H.R., J.G.M. and J.C.C. critically revised the manuscript. All authors have read and approved the final version of the manuscript. Introduction Background and objectives 2a Scientific background and explanation of rationale 36-83 2a. o Explain the mechanism (e.g., pathophysiological, pharmacological, etc.) or theoretical framework by which the intervention is expected to cause change in the outcome 36-75 2b Specific objectives or hypotheses 76-83 Methods Trial design 3a Description of trial design (such as parallel, factorial) including allocation ratio 99-106 3b Important changes to methods after trial commencement (such as eligibility criteria), with reasons N/A Participants 4a Eligibility criteria for participants 93-95 4b Settings and locations where the data were collected 108-109, 125-126 4b. o Explain suitability of setting(s) (e.g., measuring blood pressure in clinic vs. home) where the outcome data was collected, if applicable 125 Interventions 5 The interventions for each group with sufficient details to allow replication, including how and when they were actually administered 125-128, 209-231 Outcomes 6a Completely defined pre-specified primary and secondary outcome measures, including how and when they were assessed 151-206 6a. 1 Provide a rationale for the selection of the domain for the trial's primary outcome 61-69 6a. 2 Describe the specific measurement variable (e.g., systolic blood pressure), analysis metric (e.g., change from baseline, final value, time to event), method of aggregation (e.g., mean, proportion), and the time point for each outcome 128-129, 153-154, 156-158, 169-173, 197-198, 200-202, 238-242 6a. 3 If the analysis metric for the primary outcome represents within-participant change, define and justify the minimal important change in individuals N/A (continued on next page) 1 We strongly recommend reading this statement in conjunction with the CONSORT 2010 Explanation and Elaboration for important clarifications on all the items. If relevant, we also recommend reading CONSORT extensions for cluster randomized trials, noninferiority and equivalence trials, non-pharmacological treatments, herbal interventions, and pragmatic trials. Additional extensions are forthcoming: for those and for up to date references relevant to this checklist, see www.consort-statement.org. (continued) Section/topic Item no Checklist item Reported on page no 6a. 4 If the outcome data were continuous, but were analyzed as categorical (method of aggregation), specify the cutoff values used N/A 6a. 5 If outcome assessments were performed at several time points after randomization, state the time points used for the analysis 136-137 6a. 6 If a composite outcome was used, define all individual components of the composite outcome N/A 6a. 7 Identify any outcomes that were not prespecified in a trial registry or trial protocol N/A 6a. 8 Provide a description of the study instruments used to assess the outcome (e.g., questionnaires, laboratory tests) along with reliability, validity, and responsiveness in a population similar to the study sample 125-206, 260-264 6a. 9 Describe who assessed the outcome (e.g., nurse, parent) and any qualifications or trial-specific training necessary to administer the study instruments to assess the outcome 131-132 6a. 10 Describe any processes used to promote outcome data quality during data collection (e.g., duplicate measurements) and after data collection (e.g., range checks of outcome data values), or state where these details can be found 153-154, 172-173, 198-199, 205-206 6a. o Specify whether the outcome is routinely collected during usual care or specific to purpose of trial 128-129 6b Any changes to trial outcomes after the trial commenced, with reasons N/A Sample size 7a How sample size was determined 87-92 7a. 1 Define and justify the target difference between treatment groups (e.g., the minimal important difference) 243-244, 246 7b When applicable, explanation of any interim analyses and stopping guidelines 113-115 Randomization: Sequence generation 8a Method used to generate the random allocation sequence 98-99 8b Type of randomization; details of any restriction (such as blocking and block size) 99-100 Allocation concealment mechanism 9 Mechanism used to implement the random allocation sequence (such as sequentially numbered containers), describing any steps taken to conceal the sequence until interventions were assigned 98-100 Implementation 10 Who generated the random allocation sequence, who enrolled participants, and who assigned participants to interventions 98, 99 Blinding 11a If done, who was blinded after assignment to interventions (for example, participants, care providers, those assessing outcomes) and how 131-132 11b If relevant, description of the similarity of interventions 209-231 Statistical methods 12a Statistical methods used to compare groups for primary and secondary outcomes 236-247 (continued) Section/topic Item no Checklist item Reported on page no 12a. 1 Describe any methods used to account for multiplicity in the analysis or interpretation of the primary and secondary outcomes (e.g., co-primary outcomes, same outcome assessed at multiple time points, or subgroup analyses of one outcome) 238-239 12a. 2 State and justify any criteria for excluding any outcome data from the analysis and reporting, or report that no outcome data were excluded 237-238 12a. 3 Describe methods to assess patterns of missingness (e.g., missing not at random), and describe the methods to handle missing outcome items or entire assessments (e.g., multiple imputation) 237-238 12a. 4 Provide definition of outcome analysis population relating to protocol non-adherence (e.g., as randomized analysis) 251-252 12a. o Justify inclusion of covariates/factors used for analyzing the outcome data N/A 12b Methods for additional analyses, such as subgroup analyses and adjusted analyses 238-244 Results Participant flow (a diagram is strongly recommended) 13a For each group, the numbers of participants who were randomly assigned, received intended treatment, and were analyzed for the primary outcome Fig. 2 Table 2 13b For each group, losses and exclusions after randomization, together with reasons 252-253 Recruitment 14a Dates defining the periods of recruitment and follow-up 126 14b Why the trial ended or was stopped 125-127 Baseline data 15 A table showing baseline demographic and clinical characteristics for each group Table 2 Numbers analyzed 16 For each group, number of participants (denominator) included in each analysis and whether the analysis was by original assigned groups Table 2 16a. o Describe number of missing participant-level observations at each time point by treatment group, with reasons N/A Outcomes and estimation 17a For each primary and secondary outcome, results for each group, and the estimated effect size and its precision (such as 95 % confidence interval) 268-313, Tables 2-4 17a. 1 Include results for all prespecified outcome analyses or state where results can be found if not in this report 268-313, Tables 2-4 17b For binary outcomes, presentation of both absolute and relative effect sizes is recommended N/A Ancillary analyses 18 Results of any other analyses performed, including subgroup analyses and adjusted analyses, distinguishing pre-specified from exploratory 260-313 18. 1 If there were any analyses that were not prespecified, explain why they were performed N/A R. Hammami, L. Aliani, P. Gargallo et al. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport 27 (2024) 45-56 (continued) Section/topic Item no Checklist item Reported on page no Harms 19 All important harms or unintended effects in each group (for specific guidance see CONSORT for harms) 252-253 Discussion Limitations 20 Trial limitations, addressing sources of potential bias, imprecision, and, if relevant, multiplicity of analyses 391-403 Generalizability 21 Generalizability (external validity, applicability) of the trial findings 405-421 Interpretation 22 Interpretation consistent with results, balancing benefits and harms, and considering other relevant evidence 318-390 Other information Registration 23 Registration number and name of trial registry 115-119 Protocol 24 Where the full trial protocol can be accessed, if available N/A Funding 25 Sources of funding and other support (such as supply of drugs), role of funders N/A Note: o, optional items. Appendix 2. The TIDieR (Template for Intervention Description and Replication) Checklistfoot_0 : information to include when describing an intervention and the location of the information Item number Item Where located a Primary paper (page or appendix number) Other b (details) BRIEF NAME 1. Provide the name or a phrase that describes the intervention. 1-2 ______________ WHY 2. Describe any rationale, theory, or goal of the elements essential to the intervention. 37-84 _____________ WHAT 3. Materials: Describe any physical or informational materials used in the intervention, including those provided to participants or used in intervention delivery or in training of intervention providers. Provide information on where the materials can be accessed (e.g. online appendix, URL). Table 1 _____________ 4. Procedures: Describe each of the procedures, activities, and/or processes used in the intervention, including any enabling or support activities. 126-207 _____________ (continued) Item number Item Where located a Primary paper (page or appendix number) Other b (details) WHO PROVIDED 5. For each category of intervention provider (e.g. psychologist, nursing assistant), describe their expertise, background and any specific training given. 128-131 _____________ HOW 6. Describe the modes of delivery (e.g. face-to-face or by some other mechanism, such as internet or telephone) of the intervention and whether it was provided individually or in a group. 131 _____________ WHERE 7. Describe the type(s) of location(s) where the intervention occurred, including any necessary infrastructure or relevant features. 126-127 _____________ WHEN and HOW MUCH 8. Describe the number of times the intervention was delivered and over what period of time including the number of sessions, their schedule, and their duration, intensity or dose. 127, 133-135, 210-232 _____________ TAILORING 9. If the intervention was planned to be personalized, titrated or adapted, then describe what, why, when, and how. _____________ _____________ MODIFICATIONS 10. c If the intervention was modified during the course of the study, describe the changes (what, why, when, and how). N/A _____________ HOW WELL 11. Planned: If intervention adherence or fidelity was assessed, describe how and by whom, and if any strategies were used to maintain or improve fidelity, describe them. 133 _____________ 12. c Actual: If intervention adherence or fidelity was assessed, describe the extent to which the intervention was delivered as planned.
a Authors -use N/A if an item is not applicable for the intervention being described.
Reviewers -use '?' if information about the element is not reported/not sufficiently reported.

b If the information is not provided in the primary paper, give details of where this information is available. This may include locations such as a published protocol or other published papers (provide citation details) or a website (provide the URL). c If completing the TIDieR checklist for a protocol, these items are not relevant to the protocol and cannot be described until the study is complete.

figure.head: Fig. 1 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1. Schematic description of the three balance exercise types. a Static balance test: unipedal stance on stable surface, b quasi-dynamic balance test: unipedal stance test on unstable surface, and c dynamic balance test: jumping and stabilizing in unipedal stance on unstable surface.

figure.head: 4. 2 .Fig. 2 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2. Participant flow through the study.

figure.head: Fig. 3 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 3. Individual and mean pre-and post-testing data of static balance performance (CoP SA OE) by SBT, QDBT DBT and CON. SBT = static Balance Training group; QDBT = Quasi-Dynamic Balance Training group; DBT = Dynamic Balance Training group; CON = control group; d: effect size.

figure.head: Fig. 4 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 4. Individual and mean pre-and post-testing data of power performance (SLJ) by SBT, QDBT DBT and CON. SLJ: standing long jump, SBT = static Balance Training group; QDBT = Quasi-Dynamic Balance Training group; DBT = dynamic Balance Training group; CON = control group; d: effect size.

figure.head: Table 1
figure.figDesc: Description of the static, quasi-dynamic and dynamic balance-training programs..Hammami, L. Aliani, P. Gargallo et al. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport 27 (2024) 45-56 respectively (Table 1). However, all groups executed the same training volume (i.e., number of exercises, number of sets per exercise). Training progression was applied in all exercise groups, for instance changing from bipedal stance to step, tandem, and unipedal stance. In addition, the visual input was manipulated as the children progressed over the training period (e.g., eyes opened vs. closed). SBT, QDBT, and DBT replaced portions of the regular physical education curriculum. The standard physical education class lasted for 60 min and included a 15-minute warmup followed by various activities such as running, jogging, playing ball, and engaging in competitive games promoting physical movement, such as basketball, handball, and football. During the intervention, the control group participated in traditional outdoor physical education games throughout the intervention period, which included free games with fewer defined rules and techniques, such as catch and cook and hide-and-seek. The training volume was consistent across all four groups. Each training session concluded with a 15-minute cool-down, incorporating flexibility exercises and low-intensity jogging. All training sessions ended with a 15-min cool-down, that included flexibility exercises and jogging at a low intensity.

row.cell: a) Single leg stance on right
stable surface and right leg and
the hands in sagittal plane on
b) Single leg stance on left leg
stable surface and left leg and
stable surface on right leg and
stable surface on left leg and
a) Single leg stance on right
pad with eyes opened and on
balance pad and right leg and
of the hands in sagittal plane
pad with eyes opened and on
b) Single leg stance on left leg
balance pad and left leg and
balance pad on right leg and
balance pad on left leg and
single leg stance on right leg
with eyes opened and on right
balance pad and right leg and
with eyes opened and on left
single leg stance on left leg and
balance pad and left leg and
balance pad on right leg and
balance pad on left leg and

note.p: Note: Bold font indicates differences in task difficulty. SBT, balance training using a static balance exercise task; QDBT, balance training using a quasi-dynamic balance exercise task; DBT, balance training using a dynamic balance exercise task. R

figure.head: Table 2
figure.figDesc: Anthropometric characteristics of the study participants.

row.cell: 1) Values are means and standard deviations (SD); SBT, Static Balance Training group; QDBT, Quasi Dynamic Balance Training group; DBT, Dynamic Balance Training group; CON, control
group; BM, body mass; BF%, body fat percent; PHV, peak height velocity; APHV, years at peak height velocity.

figure.head: Table 3
figure.figDesc: Test-retest reliability of the applied power and balance tests..Hammami, L. Aliani, P. Gargallo et al. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport 27 (2024) 45-56

note.p: ICC, intra-class correlation coefficient; SEM, standard error of measurements; CV (%), coefficient of variation percent; CoP SA OE, center of pressure surface area with opened eyes; CoP SA CE, center of pressure surface area with closed eyes; CoP V CE, center of pressure velocity with closed eyes; SLJ, standing long jump; 3 hop jump; CMJ height, countermovement jump height. R

figure.head: Table 4
figure.figDesc: Effects of static, quasi-dynamic and dynamic balance training programs on measures of balance and power performance in children.

note.p: Note: SBT, Static Balance Training group; QDBT, Quasi-Dynamic Balance Training group; DBT, Dynamic Balance Training group; CON, Control group; CoP SA OE, center of pressure surface area with opened eyes; CoP SA CE, center of pressure surface area with closed eyes; CoP V CE, center of pressure velocity with closed eyes; SLJ, standing long jump; CMJ height, countermovement jump height.
We strongly recommend using this checklist in conjunction with the TIDieR guide (see BMJ 2014;

348: g1687) which contains an explanation and elaboration for each item. The focus of TIDieR is on reporting details of the intervention elements (and where relevant, comparison elements) of a study. Other elements and methodological features of studies are covered by other reporting statements and checklists and have not been duplicated as part of the TIDieR checklist. When a randomized trial is being reported, the TIDieR checklist should be used in conjunction with the CONSORT statement (see www.consortstatement.org) as an extension of Item 5 of the CONSORT 2010 Statement. When a clinical trial protocol is being reported, the TIDieR checklist should be used in conjunction with the SPIRIT statement as an extension of Item 11 of the SPIRIT 2013 Statement (see www.spiritstatement.org). For alternate study designs, TIDieR can be used in conjunction with the appropriate checklist for that study design (see www.equator-network.org). R. Hammami, L. Aliani, P. Gargallo et al. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport 27 (2024) 45-56

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: We would like to thank the participants and the research assistants involved in the data collection.

div.head: Funding information
div.p: No funding.

div.head: Confirmation of ethical compliance
div.p: The study was conducted according to the latest version of the Declaration of Helsinki and the protocol was fully approved by the Local Clinical Research Ethics Committee "Personal Protection Committee" under the following code (N°: 0365//2021) before the commencement of the assessments. Written informed consent to participate in this study was provided by the participants' legal guardian/next of kin.

div.head: Declaration of interest statement
div.p: The authors declare that the research was conducted in the absence of any commercial or financial relationships that could be construed as a potential conflict of interest.

analytic.title: Exploring of two different equated instability resistance training programs on measure of physical fitness and lower limb asymmetry in pre-pubertal weightlifters

biblStruct.note: Hammami R, Nobari H, Hanen W et al. Exploring of two different equated in- stability resistance training programs on measure of physical fitness and lower limb asymmetry in pre-pubertal weightlifters. BMC Sports Sci Med Rehabil 2023;15(1):40.

analytic.title: What to train first: balance or explosive strength? Impact on performance and intracortical inhibition

biblStruct.note: Lauber B, Gollhofer A, Taube W. What to train first: balance or explosive strength? Im- pact on performance and intracortical inhibition. Scand J Med Sci Sports 2021;31(6): 1301-1312.

analytic.title: Effects of balance training on physical fitness in youth and young athletes: a narrative review

biblStruct.note: Gebel A, Prieske O, Behm DG et al. Effects of balance training on physical fitness in youth and young athletes: a narrative review. Strength Cond J 2020;42(6):35-44.

analytic.title: Effects of balance training on balance performance in youth: role of training difficulty

biblStruct.note: Schedler S, Tenelsen F, Wich L et al. Effects of balance training on balance perfor- mance in youth: role of training difficulty. BMC Sports Sci Med Rehabil 2020;12(1):71.

analytic.title: Is young age a limiting factor when training balance? Effects of child-oriented balance training in children and adolescents

biblStruct.note: Wälchli M, Ruffieux J, Mouthon A et al. Is young age a limiting factor when training balance? Effects of child-oriented balance training in children and adolescents. Pediatr Exerc Sci 2018;30(1):176-184.

analytic.title: Intensity of challenge to the balance system is not reported in the prescription of balance exercises in randomised trials: a systematic review

biblStruct.note: Farlie MK, Robins L, Keating JL et al. Intensity of challenge to the balance system is not reported in the prescription of balance exercises in randomised trials: a system- atic review. J Physiother 2013;59(4):227-235.

analytic.title: Development of postural control and maturation of sensory systems in children of different ages a cross-sectional study

biblStruct.note: de Sá CSC, Boffino CC, Ramos RT et al. Development of postural control and matura- tion of sensory systems in children of different ages a cross-sectional study. Braz J Phys Ther 2018;22(1):70-76.

analytic.title: Development of lower extremity kinetics for balance control in infants and young children

biblStruct.note: Roncesvalles MN, Woollacott MH, Jensen JL. Development of lower extremity kinetics for balance control in infants and young children. J Mot Behav 2001;33(2):180-192.

analytic.title: The development of postural strategies in children: a factorial design study

biblStruct.note: Schmid M, Conforto S, Lopez L et al. The development of postural strategies in chil- dren: a factorial design study. J Neuroeng Rehabil 2005;2(1):29.

analytic.title: Non-linear development of postural control and strategy use in young children: a longitudinal study

biblStruct.note: Kirshenbaum N, Riach CL, Starkes JL. Non-linear development of postural control and strategy use in young children: a longitudinal study. Exp Brain Res 2001;140(4):420-431.

analytic.title: The growth of stability: postural control from a developmental perspective

biblStruct.note: Shumway-Cook A, Woollacott MH. The growth of stability: postural control from a developmental perspective. J Mot Behav 1985;17(2):131-147.

analytic.title: Associations between balance and muscle strength, power performance in male youth athletes of different maturity status

biblStruct.note: Hammami R, Chaouachi A, Makhlouf I et al. Associations between balance and mus- cle strength, power performance in male youth athletes of different maturity status. Pediatr Exerc Sci 2016;28(4):521-534.

analytic.title: Single-and dual-task balance training are equally effective in youth

biblStruct.note: Luder B, Kiss R, Granacher U. Single-and dual-task balance training are equally effec- tive in youth. Front Psychol 2018;9:912.

analytic.title: One-leg standing performance and muscle activity: are there limb differences

biblStruct.note: Muehlbauer T, Mettler C, Roth R et al. One-leg standing performance and muscle ac- tivity: are there limb differences? J Appl Biomech 2014;30(3):407-414.

analytic.title: Programme frequency, type, time and duration do not explain the effects of balance exercise in older adults: a systematic review with a meta-regression analysis

biblStruct.note: Farlie MK, Robins L, Haas R et al. Programme frequency, type, time and duration do not explain the effects of balance exercise in older adults: a systematic review with a meta-regression analysis. Br J Sports Med 2019;53(16):996-1002.

analytic.title: Instability training, assessing the impact of level of difficulty on balance: a randomized clinical trial

biblStruct.note: Blasco JM, Tolsada C, Beltran M et al. Instability training, assessing the impact of level of difficulty on balance: a randomized clinical trial. Gait Posture 2019;70:116-121.

analytic.title: Acute effects of different balance exercise types on selected measures of physical fitness in youth female volleyball players

biblStruct.note: Hammami R, Chaabene H, Kharrat F et al. Acute effects of different balance exercise types on selected measures of physical fitness in youth female volleyball players. BMC Sports Sci Med Rehabil 2021;13(1):29.

analytic.title: Effects of task difficulty during practice on learning a dynamic balance task in healthy young adults: an intervention study

biblStruct.note: Schedler S, Leifeld P, Seidel T et al. Effects of task difficulty during practice on learning a dynamic balance task in healthy young adults: an intervention study. BMC Res Notes 2021;14(1):235.

analytic.title: Challenging balance during sensorimotor adaptation increases generalization

biblStruct.note: Bakkum A, Donelan JM, Marigold DS. Challenging balance during sensorimotor adap- tation increases generalization. J Neurophysiol 2020;123(4):1342-1354.

analytic.title: Specificity of balance training in healthy individuals: a systematic review and meta-analysis

biblStruct.note: Kummel J, Kramer A, Giboin LS et al. Specificity of balance training in healthy individ- uals: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Sports Med 2016;46(9):1261-1271.

analytic.title: Cortical, subcortical and spinal neural correlates of slackline training-induced balance performance improvements

biblStruct.note: Giboin LS, Loewe K, Hassa T et al. Cortical, subcortical and spinal neural correlates of slackline training-induced balance performance improvements. Neuroimage 2019;202:116061.

analytic.title: The assessment of the body fat percentage by skinfold thickness measurements in childhood and young adolescence

biblStruct.note: Deurenberg P, Pieters JJ, Hautvast JG. The assessment of the body fat percentage by skinfold thickness measurements in childhood and young adolescence. Br J Nutr 1990;63(2):293-303.

analytic.title: Enhancing a somatic maturity prediction model

biblStruct.note: Moore SA, McKay HA, Macdonald H et al. Enhancing a somatic maturity prediction model. Med Sci Sports Exerc 2015;47(8):1755-1764.

analytic.title: A comparative analysis of the center of gravity and center of pressure trajectory path lengths in standing posture: an estimation of active stiffness

biblStruct.note: Caron O, Gelat T, Rougier P et al. A comparative analysis of the center of gravity and center of pressure trajectory path lengths in standing posture: an estimation of active stiffness. J Appl Biomech 2000;16(3):234-247.
Porac C, Coren S. Lateral preferences and human behavior, Springer, 1981.

analytic.title: Competitive performance of elite track-and-field athletes: variability and smallest worthwhile enhancements

analytic.title: Effects of resistance training in youth athletes on muscular fitness and athletic performance: a conceptual model for long-term athlete development

biblStruct.note: Hulsdunker T, Mierau A, Struder HK. Higher balance task demands are associated with an increase in individual alpha peak frequency. Front Hum Neurosci 2015;9:695.
